Quantitative Aptitude.title: Quantitative Aptitude
Quantitative Aptitude.overview: Quantitative aptitude tests your ability to solve basic mathematical problems. Select a sub-topic to see detailed formulas.

Percentage.title: 1. Fundamental Concepts
Percentage.content:
Percentage is derived from the Latin word 'percentum', meaning 'for every hundred'. It is a fraction whose denominator is 100.

Basic Conversions:
  • Percent to Fraction: x% = x/100
  • Fraction to Percent: (x/y) = (x/y × 100)%
  • Percent to Decimal: x% = x/100 = 0.01x
Expressing One Quantity w.r.t Another:
Percentage = [ (Quantity to be expressed) / (Standard Quantity) ] × 100

Note: Both quantities must be in the same unit.

Percentage.title: Solved Examples
Percentage.content:
Example 1: Convert 4% to a fraction. (CISF Head Constable 2023)

Solution:
4% = 4/100 = 1/25

Example 2: What is 2/5 expressed as a percentage? (RRB NTPC 2021)

Solution:
2/5 × 100 = 40%

Example 3: If x% of y is equal to 2/3 of 90, then find the value of xy. (SSC GD 2023)

Solution:
(x/100) × y = 2/3 × 90
xy/100 = 60 ⇒ xy = 6000

Example 4: 0.5 is what percentage of 20? (SSC CGL 2022)

Solution:
(0.5/20) × 100 = 2.5%

Percentage.title: 2. Comparison Techniques
Percentage.content:
Technique 1: If X is a% more than Y, then Y is less than X by:

[ a / (100 + a) ] × 100 %

If X is a% less than Y, then Y is more than X by:

[ a / (100 - a) ] × 100 %

Percentage.title: 3. Successive Changes
Percentage.content:
Technique 2: Equal Increase then Decrease

If a value is first increased by a% and then decreased by a%, the net effect is always a decrease of:

(a/10)² % or (a²/100) %

Technique 3: General Successive Change

If a value changes by a% and then by b%, the net change is:

[ ± a ± b + (ab/100) ] %

Use (+) for increase and (-) for decrease.

Percentage.title: 4. Expenditure & Consumption
Percentage.content:
Technique 4: Price vs. Consumption

If the price of a commodity increases or decreases by a%, the change in consumption required to keep expenditure constant is:

[ a / (100 ± a) ] × 100 %

Technique 5: Quantity for Fixed Price

If a price change of r% allows a person to buy A kg more or less for ₹X:

  • Reduced Price: (rX / 100A) per kg
  • Original Price: (rX / (100 ± r)A) per kg

Percentage.title: 5. Examination & Marks
Percentage.content:
Technique 6: Maximum Marks

If a candidate scores R marks, fails by F marks, and the passing percentage is P%:

Maximum Marks (M) = [ 100(R + F) ] / P

Technique 7: Two Candidates Comparison

If candidate A scores x% and fails by 'a' marks, while candidate B scores y% and gets 'b' marks more than pass marks:

Maximum Marks (M) = [ 100(a + b) ] / (y - x)

Percentage.title: 6. Population & Depreciation
Percentage.content:
Technique 8: Constant Rate

If population P increases at R% per annum:

  • After n years: P(1 + R/100)n
  • n years ago: P / (1 + R/100)n

Use (-) for decrease or depreciation.

Technique 9: Variable Rates

Population after 3 years with different rates (R1, R2, R3):

Final = P(1 ± R1/100)(1 ± R2/100)(1 ± R3/100)

Percentage.title: 7. Important Fraction-to-Percentage Table
Percentage.content:
FractionPercentageMixed Fraction
1/250%50%
1/333.33%33 1/3%
1/425%25%
1/616.66%16 2/3%
1/714.28%14 2/7%
1/812.5%12 1/2%
1/119.09%9 1/11%

Profit & Loss.title: 1. Basic Concepts
Profit & Loss.content:
Introduction

Profit and loss are crucial terms related to financial transactions in business and trade. Whenever a purchased or manufactured item is sold, either profit is earned or loss is incurred.

Key Terms

  • Cost Price (CP): The price at which an article or item is purchased or manufactured.
  • Selling Price (SP): The price at which an article is sold.
  • Profit (P): Earned when the Selling Price (SP) is more than the Cost Price (CP). Also referred to as 'Gain'.
  • Loss (L): Incurred when the Selling Price (SP) is less than the Cost Price (CP).
  • Marked Price (MP): The price at which an item is listed for sale. Also known as Labeled Price or Tag Price.
  • Overhead Expense: Extra expenditure (rent, transport, etc.) on purchased goods apart from actual CP. These are always added to the Cost Price.

Profit & Loss.title: 2. Important Notes
Profit & Loss.content:
• There is neither profit nor loss if an item is sold at its Cost Price.
  • Overhead expenses must be added to the CP before calculating profit or loss.
  • Profit % and Loss % are always calculated on the Cost Price (CP) unless stated otherwise.

Profit & Loss.title: 3. Essential Formulas
Profit & Loss.content:
Basic Formulas:
Profit (Gain) = SP - CP
Loss = CP - SP
Percentage Formulas:
Gain % = (Gain / CP) × 100
Loss % = (Loss / CP) × 100
Finding Selling Price (SP):
SP = [(100 + Gain%) / 100] × CP
SP = [(100 - Loss%) / 100] × CP
Finding Cost Price (CP):
CP = [100 / (100 + Gain%)] × SP
CP = [100 / (100 - Loss%)] × SP

Profit & Loss.title: 4. Solved Examples
Profit & Loss.content:
Example 1 : A salesman expects a gain of 13% on his cost price. If in a month, his sale was ₹7,91,000, then what was his profit? (CUET 2023)

Solution:
Gain = 13%, SP = ₹7,91,000
CP = (SP × 100) / (100 + Gain%) = (7,91,000 × 100) / 113 = ₹7,00,000
Profit = SP - CP = 7,91,000 - 7,00,000 = ₹91,000

Example 2 : If the selling price of a toy is 3/5 of its cost price, then the loss per cent is: (MHA IB 2023)

Solution:
Let CP = x. Then SP = 3/5x.
Loss = CP - SP = x - (3/5)x = (2/5)x
Loss% = (Loss / CP) × 100 = [(2/5)x / x] × 100 = 2/5 × 100 = 40%

Example 3 : If an article is sold at 20% profit, what is the ratio of CP to SP? (HCET 2022)

Solution:
Let CP = 100. Profit = 20% ⇒ SP = 120.
Ratio CP : SP = 100 : 120 = 5 : 6

Example 4 : When a plot is sold for ₹18,700, the owner loses 15%. At what price must it be sold to gain 15%? (HCET 2022)

Solution:
SP1 = ₹18,700, Loss = 15%.
CP = (18,700 × 100) / 85 = ₹22,000.
New SP for 15% Gain = 22,000 × 1.15 = ₹25,300

Profit & Loss.title: 5. Advanced Techniques
Profit & Loss.content:
Technique 1: Common Gain and Loss

Condition: When two items are sold at the same Selling Price (SP), one at a gain of a% and another at a loss of a%.

Loss % = (a / 10)² %

Note: In this specific case, the seller always incurs a loss, and the actual SP value is immaterial to the percentage calculation.

Technique 2: Relation between CP and SP quantity

Condition: If the Cost Price (CP) of 'a' articles is equal to the Selling Price (SP) of 'b' articles.

Profit/Loss % = [(a - b) / b] × 100%

Note: A positive result indicates profit; a negative result indicates loss.

Technique 3: Price and Quantity Variations

Condition: If 'm' items are purchased for ₹x and 'n' items are sold for ₹y.

Profit/Loss % = [(my - nx) / nx] × 100%

Note: Cross-multiply items and prices: (Items_Buy * Price_Sell - Items_Sell * Price_Buy) / (Items_Sell * Price_Buy).

Technique 4: Change in Selling Price

Condition: A man sells an item at a% profit/loss. If he had sold it for ₹R more, he would have gained/lost b%.

CP = [R / (b ± a)] × 100

Note: Use '-' if both cases are the same (both profit or both loss). Use '+' if one is profit and the other is loss.

Technique 5: Successive Transactions

Condition: If A sells to B at r1% profit/loss, and B sells to C at r2% profit/loss.

CP for C = CP for A × [1 ± (r1/100)] × [1 ± (r2/100)]

Note: Use '+' for profit and '-' for loss.

Technique 6: Dishonest Dealer (False Weights)

Condition: A trader sells at CP but uses a false weight.

Gain % = [Error / (True Value - Error)] × 100%

Alternatively:

[ (True Weight - False Weight) / False Weight ] × 100%

Technique 7: Loss on CP with False Weights

Condition: If a shopkeeper sells at a% loss on CP but uses 'b' grams instead of 'c' grams.

Profit/Loss % = [ (100 - a) × (c / b) ] - 100

Note: Positive result = Profit; Negative result = Loss.

Technique 8: Combined Profit/Loss and Weight Fraud

Condition: A dealer sells at a% profit/loss on CP and uses b% less weight.

Profit/Loss % = [ (b ± a) / (100 - b) ] × 100%

Note: Use '+a' for profit and '-a' for loss.

Profit & Loss.title: 6. Advanced Examples
Profit & Loss.content:
Technique-Based Examples

Example 5 : A shopkeeper sells two articles at ₹840 each. He earns 20% profit on the first and 20% loss on the second. Net profit/loss? (UP Constable 2023)

Solution:
Apply Technique 1: Loss % = (a/10)² = (20/10)² = 4% Loss

Example 6 : The SP of 10 articles equals the CP of 13 articles. Gain %? (MTS 2023)

Solution:
Apply Technique 2: Gain % = [(13-10)/10] × 100 = 30%

Example 7 : A vendor bought bananas at 6 for ₹10 and sold at 4 for ₹6. Gain/Loss %? (UP Constable 2024)

Solution:
Apply Technique 3: [(6×6 - 4×10) / 40] × 100 = -4/40 × 100 = 10% Loss

Example 8 : An article was sold at 16% loss. If sold for ₹660 more, gain would be 8%. SP for 12% gain? (SSC CPO 2020)

Solution:
Apply Technique 4: CP = [660 / (16+8)] × 100 = ₹2750. SP for 12% Gain = 2750 × 1.12 = ₹3080

Example 9 : A sells to B at 33% profit, B to C at 20% loss. C pays ₹532. CP for A? (SSC CHSL 2024)

Solution:
Apply Technique 5: 532 = CP × 1.33 × 0.8 ⇒ CP = 532 / 1.064 = ₹500

Example 10 : A vendor weighs 850gm for 1000gm. Profit %? (RRB Group D 2022)

Solution:
Apply Technique 6: [(1000-850)/850] × 100 = 17.65%

Example 11 : Dealer sells at 6% loss on CP but uses 14gm for 16gm. Profit/Loss %? (AFCAT 2021)

Solution:
Apply Technique 7: [(100-6) × (16/14)] - 100 = 7 3/7% Profit

Example 12 : Rice sold at 10% profit using 20% less weight. Gain %? (SSC GD 2023)

Solution:
Apply Technique 8: [(20+10)/(100-20)] × 100 = 37.5%

Simple and Compound Interest.title: Simple Interest
Simple and Compound Interest.content: Simple Interest (SI) is calculated only on the principal amount. SI = (P × R × T) / 100.

Simple and Compound Interest.title: Compound Interest
Simple and Compound Interest.content: Compound Interest (CI) is calculated on the principal and also on the accumulated interest. CI = P(1 + R/100)^T - P.

Ratio and Proportion.title: 1. Ratio Basics
Ratio and Proportion.content:
Think of a ratio as the 'language of comparison.' It is a simple way to see how much bigger or smaller one thing is compared to another.

In a ratio like x : y,
'x' is the Antecedent (the leader)
'y' is the Consequent (the follower)

The Golden Rule: Always compare 'apples to apples.' You can compare weights to weights, but you cannot create a ratio between different units like apples and kilometers.

Ratio and Proportion.title: 2. Ratio Upgrades
Ratio and Proportion.content:
• Duplicate Ratio: Square both numbers.
    Example: 3:4 becomes 9:16.
  • Triplicate Ratio: Cube both numbers.
    Example: 2:3 becomes 8:27.
  • Inverse Ratio: Swap their positions.
    Example: 4:5 becomes 5:4.

Ratio and Proportion.title: 3. Comparison Shortcut
Ratio and Proportion.content:
Which is 'Better'?

The Cross-Multiply Shortcut: To compare 5/8 and 9/14:

1. Multiply top-left by bottom-right: 5 × 14 = 70.
2. Multiply bottom-left by top-right: 8 × 9 = 72.
3. Since 72 is bigger than 70, the second ratio (9/14) is greater.

Ratio and Proportion.title: 4. Proportion Rules
Ratio and Proportion.content:
A proportion is just a fancy way of saying two ratios are perfectly equal (a/b = c/d).

The Secret Property: The 'Outsiders' multiplied together always equal the 'Insiders' (a × d = b × c).

Finding Missing Values

  • Mean Proportional: Square root of (a × b).
    Example: The middle of 4 and 25 is √100 = 10.
  • Fourth Proportional: (b × c) / a.
    Example: For 3, 7, and 9, the missing piece is (7 × 9) / 3 = 21.

Ratio and Proportion.title: 5. Pro Tricks
Ratio and Proportion.content:
• Componendo: Add the bottom number to the top: (a + b) / b.
  • Dividendo: Subtract the bottom number from the top: (a - b) / b.
  • The C&D Combo: If a/b = 5/3, then (5+3) / (5-3) = 8/2 = 4.

Ratio and Proportion.title: 6. Beginner Cheat Sheet
Ratio and Proportion.content:
TermMeaningFormula
RatioComparing two thingsx : y
InverseFlipping the ordery : x
ProportionTwo equal ratiosa:b :: c:d
Mean ProportionalFinding the middle√(ab)
InvertendoFlipping both sidesb/a = d/c

Ratio and Proportion.title: 7. Advance Techniques
Ratio and Proportion.content:
Technique 1: Combining Multiple Ratios

Used to find a single relationship when you have overlapping ratios.

If A:B = a:b and B:C = m:n, then A:B:C = (am) : (mb) : (nb)

Technique 2: Division of a Quantity

To find a part, take that part's ratio, divide by the sum of all ratios, and multiply by the total.

Part 1 = [a / (a+b)] × Total

Technique 3: Income and Expenditure

Shortcut for finding income when savings are equal.

Income = [Savings × (Diff in Exp. Ratios) / (Cross Product)] × Individual Ratio.

Technique 4 & 5: Adding/Subtracting Numbers

Used when adding or subtracting a constant x to two numbers changes their ratio from a:b to c:d.

Number = [x × a(d - c)] / (bc - ad)

Averages.title: 1. Basic Concepts & Formula
Averages.content:
An average or an arithmetic mean of given data is the sum of the given observations divided by the number of observations.

Average (A) = Sum of observations (S) / Number of observations (N)
Symbolically: A = S / N

Averages.title: 2. Properties & Rules
Averages.content:
Key Properties

  • Range: The average of a given data set is less than the greatest observation and greater than the smallest observation.
  • Equality: If all observations are equal, the average will be the same as the observations.
  • Zero as an Observation: If 0 is one of the observations, it must be included while calculating the average.

Arithmetic Rules

  • If all numbers increase by 'a', the average increases by 'a'.
  • If all numbers decrease by 'a', the average decreases by 'a'.
  • If all numbers are multiplied by 'a', the average is multiplied by 'a'.
  • If all numbers are divided by 'a', the average is divided by 'a'.

Averages.title: 3. Important Formulae
Averages.content:
• Average of first 'n' natural numbers = (n + 1) / 2
  • Average of first 'n' even numbers = n + 1
  • Average of first 'n' odd numbers = n
  • Average of consecutive numbers = (First number + Last number) / 2
  • Average of 1 to 'n' odd numbers = (Last odd number + 1) / 2
  • Average of 1 to 'n' even numbers = (Last even number + 2) / 2
  • Average of squares of first 'n' natural numbers = [(n + 1)(2n + 1)] / 6
  • Average of cubes of first 'n' natural numbers = [n(n + 1)²] / 4
  • Average of 'n' multiples of a number = [Number × (n + 1)] / 2

Averages.title: 4. Solved Examples
Averages.content:
Example 1: Find out the average of 308, 125, 45, 120 and 102.

Solution: Required average = (308 + 125 + 45 + 120 + 102) / 5 = 700 / 5 = 140

Example 2: If the weight of A is 60 kg, B is 45 kg and C is 54 kg, what is the average weight of the three persons?

Solution: Required average = (60 + 45 + 54) / 3 = 159 / 3 = 53 kg

Example 3: Chandan's average expenditure for 4 days is ₹90. If the first 3 days were ₹100, ₹125 and ₹85, find the 4th day expenditure.

Solution: Let 4th day be x. (100 + 125 + 85 + x) / 4 = 90 ⇒ 310 + x = 360 ⇒ x = ₹50

Example 4: What will be the average of numbers from 1 to 51?

Solution: n = 51. Formula: (n + 1) / 2 = (51 + 1) / 2 = 26

Example 5: Find out the average of 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12 and 14.

Solution: n = 7. Formula: (n + 1) = (7 + 1) = 8

Example 6: Calculate the average of 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13, 15 and 17.

Solution: n = 9. Formula: n = 9

Example 7: What will be the average of 1, 2, 3, 4, ..., 51, 52, 53?

Solution: First = 1, Last = 53. Formula: (1 + 53) / 2 = 27

Example 8: Find out the average of 4, 7, 10, 13, ..., 28, 31.

Solution: Common difference is 3. First = 4, Last = 31. Average = (4 + 31) / 2 = 17.5

Example 9: Find the average of all odd and even numbers from 1 to 45.

Solution: Odd: (45 + 1) / 2 = 23. Even: (44 + 2) / 2 = 23

Example 10: Calculate the average of the squares of natural numbers from 1 to 25.

Solution: n = 25. Formula: [(n + 1)(2n + 1)] / 6 = (26 × 51) / 6 = 221

Example 11: Calculate the average of the cubes of the first 9 natural numbers.

Solution: n = 9. Formula: [n(n + 1)²] / 4 = [9 × 100] / 4 = 225

Example 12: What will be the average of the first 9 multiples of 5?

Solution: n = 9, Number = 5. Formula: [Number × (n + 1)] / 2 = [5 × 10] / 2 = 25

Averages.title: 5. Advance Techniques
Averages.content:
Advanced Exam Formulas

Technique 1: Weighted Average

Used when different groups have different averages.

Average = (n1a1 + n2a2 + ...) / (n1 + n2 + ...)

Technique 2: Average of Remaining Observations

Remaining Average = (ma - nb) / (m - n)

Technique 3: Addition or Elimination of Observation

Eliminated Value = n(a - b) + b
Added Value = n(b - a) + b

Technique 4: Replacement of Observations

New Value = Replaced Value ± (n × Change in Avg)

Technique 5: Passed vs Failed Students

Passed Students = [n(Total Avg - Failed Avg)] / (Passed Avg - Failed Avg)

Technique 6: Group Components (Officers/Non-officers)

Remaining Components = [n(Group Avg - Part1 Avg)] / (Part2 Avg - Group Avg)

Technique 7: Average Speed

  • Two equal distances: 2AB / (A + B)
  • Three equal distances: 3ABC / (AB + BC + CA)
  • Different distances: Total Distance / Total Time

Problems Based on Ages.title: 1. Basic Concept
Problems Based on Ages.content:
Age is defined as a period of time that a person has lived or a thing has existed. It is generally measured in months, years, or decades.[cite: 1]

Problems based on ages usually provide a relationship between the ages of two or more people in the present, future, or past to calculate a missing value.[cite: 1]

Problems Based on Ages.title: 2. Fundamental Rules
Problems Based on Ages.content:
Rule 1: Past Ratios

If the ratio of present ages of A and B is x : y and n yr ago, the ratio was p : q:[cite: 1]

(x - n) / (y - n) = p / q

Rule 2: Future Ratios

If the ratio of present ages of A and B is x : y and after n yr, the ratio will be p : q:[cite: 2]

(x + n) / (y + n) = p / q
Note: Most age problems can be solved effectively using linear equations.[cite: 2]

Problems Based on Ages.title: 3. Fast Track Techniques
Problems Based on Ages.content:
Technique 1: Present vs. Future Multiples

If at present a person is m times as old as another, and after t years they become n times as old:[cite: 3]

Age of first person = tm [(n - 1) / (m - n)]
Age of second person = t [(n - 1) / (m - n)]

Technique 2: Past vs. Future Multiples

If t1 yr ago, age was m times, and after t2 yr, age will be n times:[cite: 4]

First Person = [t2m(n - 1) + t1n(m - 1)] / (m - n)
Second Person = [t2(n - 1) + t1(m - 1)] / (m - n)

Technique 3: The Relationship Rule

If person M is as much older to N as he is younger to P, and the sum of ages of N and P is S:

M's age = S / 2

Problems Based on Ages.title: 4. Solved Examples
Problems Based on Ages.content:
Example 1: A father is twice as old as his son. 20 yr back, he was twelve times as old as his son. What is the father's present age?[cite: 1]

Solution: Let son = k, father = 2k.
(2k - 20) / (k - 20) = 12 / 1
⇒ 2k - 20 = 12k - 240
⇒ 10k = 220 ⇒ k = 22.
Father = 2 × 22 = 44 yr

Example 2: I am 3 times as old as my son. 15 yr hence, I will be twice as old as my son. Sum of our present ages?[cite: 2]

Solution: Let son = k, man = 3k.
(3k + 15) / (k + 15) = 2 / 1
⇒ 3k + 15 = 2k + 30 ⇒ k = 15.
Sum = 3k + k = 4k = 4 × 15 = 60 yr

Example 3: 5 yr ago, Sachin was 4 times the age of Vineet. After 10 yr, he will be twice as old. Find Sachin's age.[cite: 4]

Solution: t1=5, t2=10, m=4, n=2.
Sachin = [5×2(4-1) + 10×4(2-1)] / (4-2)
= [30 + 40] / 2 = 35 yr

Example 4: Akshay is as much older than Vinay as he is younger to Kartik. Sum of Vinay and Kartik's age is 48. Find Akshay's age.

Solution: Akshay = Sum / 2
= 48 / 2 = 24 yr

Work and Time.title: 1. Introduction and Important Relations
Work and Time.content:
This chapter explores techniques to solve problems based on work completion time and the number of persons required. A central concept is that while solving these problems, the total work done is always supposed to be equal to 1.

Core Proportionality Relations

  • Work and Person: Directly proportional (more work requires more men; more men can do more work).
  • Time and Person: Inversely proportional (more men requires less time; more time requires fewer men).
  • Work and Time: Directly proportional (more work requires more time; more time allows for more work).

Work and Time.title: 2. Basic Rules and Examples
Work and Time.content:
Rule 1 & 2: Unitary Work Method

  • If a person can do a piece of work in n days (hours), then that person's 1 day's (hour's) work = 1/n.
  • If a person's 1 day's (hour's) work = 1/n, then the person will complete the work in n days (hours).
Ex. 1: Vandana completes a work in 35 days. What work will she do in 1 day?
Sol: Required work done = 1/n = 1/35.
Ex. 2: Kavi does 1/13 part of work in 1 day. In how many days will he complete the whole work?
Sol: Required number of days = 13.

Rule 3: Efficiency and Time

If a person is n times efficient than a second person:

  • Work done by First : Second = n : 1.
  • Time taken by First : Second = 1 : n.
Ex. 3: P can do a work 3 times faster than Q and takes 40 days less than Q. Find individual times.
Sol: Ratio of time P:Q = 1:3. Let times be K and 3K. 3K - K = 40 ⇒ 2K = 40 ⇒ K = 20. P = 20 days; Q = 60 days.

Rule 4: Men to Time Ratio

If ratio of numbers of men required is m : n, the ratio of time taken will be n : m.

Ex. 4: If 12 men finish a work in 20 days, how many days for 15 men?
Sol: Ratio of men = 12:15 = 4:5. Ratio of times = 5:4. Let 15 men take x days. 20:x = 5:4 ⇒ x = 16 days.

Work and Time.title: 3. Fast Track Techniques
Work and Time.content:
Technique 1: Multi-Variable Relation

If M₁ persons do W₁ work in D₁ days at T₁ hours/day, and M₂ persons do W₂ work in D₂ days at T₂ hours/day:

M₁D₁T₁W₂ = M₂D₂T₂W₁
Ex. 5: 10 men make 20 toys in 12 days (12 h/day). In how many days can 24 persons make 32 toys (16 h/day)?
Sol: 10 × 12 × 12 × 32 = 24 × D₂ × 16 × 20. Solving gives D₂ = 6 days.

Technique 2 & 3: Combined Group Work

  • If A takes x days and B takes y days, together they take: xy / (x + y) days.
  • For three persons (x, y, z), time taken = xyz / (xy + yz + zx) days.
  • If A+B together take x days and A alone takes y days, B alone takes: xy / (y - x) days.

Technique 5: 'Or' vs 'And' Efficiency

If a men OR b women take D days, then a₂ men and b₂ women take:

Time = D(a × b) / (a₂b + ab₂) days

Technique 9: Relative Extra Days

If x takes a days more than (x+y) and y takes b days more than (x+y):

Time for (x+y) = √(ab) days
Ex. 14: A takes 16 days more than (A+B), B takes 9 days more. Together?
Sol: √(16 × 9) = 4 × 3 = 12 days.

Technique 10 & 11: Work Completion & Leaving

  • If B leaves x days BEFORE completion: Total Time = (b + x)a / (a + b).
  • If A leaves and B finishes remaining work in x days: Time A worked = (b - x)a / (a + b).

Pipes and Cisterns.title: Pipes and Cisterns
Pipes and Cisterns.content: Problems involving filling and emptying tanks using pipes. Use the concept of work/time.

Time, Speed and Distance.title: The Speed-Time-Distance Relationship
Time, Speed and Distance.content:
This is the foundation of all problems involving motion. The three variables are linked by one simple equation:

Distance = Speed × Time

Unit Conversion (The 5/18 Rule):
  • To convert km/hr to m/s: Multiply by 5/18.
  • To convert m/s to km/hr: Multiply by 18/5.

Time, Speed and Distance.title: Relative Speed and Trains
Time, Speed and Distance.content:
Relative speed is the speed of one object with respect to another.

  • Same Direction: Relative Speed = |S1 - S2|
  • Opposite Direction: Relative Speed = S1 + S2

Train Crossing Logic:
1. When a train crosses a stationary pole/man, the distance covered equals the Length of the Train.
2. When a train crosses a bridge/platform, the distance covered equals (Length of Train + Length of Platform).

HCF & LCM.title: HCF and LCM Concepts
HCF & LCM.content:
• HCF (Highest Common Factor): The greatest number that exactly divides two or more numbers. Also known as GCD.
  • LCM (Least Common Multiple): The smallest number which is exactly divisible by two or more numbers.

Crucial Rule for Two Numbers:
HCF(a,b) × LCM(a,b) = a × b

Divisibility rules.title: Divisibility rules
Divisibility rules.content: Rules to quickly check if a number is divisible by another (e.g., 2, 3, 5, 9, 11).

Simplification (BODMAS).title: Simplification (BODMAS)
Simplification (BODMAS).content: Order of operations: Brackets, Orders, Division, Multiplication, Addition, Subtraction.

Number Series.title: Number Series
Number Series.content: Find the pattern in a sequence of numbers and predict the next term.

Permutation & Combination.title: Permutation & Combination
Permutation & Combination.content: Permutation: Arrangements, Combination: Selections. Use nPr and nCr formulas.

Probability.title: Fundamentals of Probability
Probability.content:
Probability is the branch of mathematics concerning numerical descriptions of how likely an event is to occur.


The Probability Formula:
P(E) = n(E) / n(S)

Where:
n(E) = Number of favorable outcomes
n(S) = Total number of possible outcomes in the Sample Space.

Mensuration (Area and Volume).title: Mensuration (Area and Volume)
Mensuration (Area and Volume).content: Formulas for area and volume of basic shapes: square, rectangle, circle, cylinder, sphere, etc.

Logical Reasoning & Mental Ability.title: Logical Reasoning & Mental Ability
Logical Reasoning & Mental Ability.overview: Logical reasoning helps in developing analytical skills through patterns and puzzles.

Syllogism.title: Syllogism
Syllogism.content: Problems involving logical deduction using statements and conclusions. Use Venn diagrams for clarity.

Blood Relations.title: Blood Relations
Blood Relations.content: Questions based on family relationships. Draw family trees for accuracy.

Coding-Decoding.title: Coding-Decoding
Coding-Decoding.content: Questions where words or numbers are coded using a pattern. Find the logic to decode.

Seating Arrangement (Linear/Circular).title: Seating Arrangement
Seating Arrangement (Linear/Circular).content: Arranging people in a line or circle based on given conditions. Visualize positions for accuracy.

Direction Sense.title: Direction Sense
Direction Sense.content: Problems involving directions (North, South, East, West) and turns. Draw diagrams to avoid confusion.

Statement and Assumptions.title: Statement and Assumptions
Statement and Assumptions.content: Identify assumptions implicit in a given statement.

Cause and Effect.title: Cause and Effect
Cause and Effect.content: Determine the cause or effect relationship between two statements.

Course of Action.title: Course of Action
Course of Action.content: Decide the best course of action based on a given situation.

Mirror and Water Images.title: Mirror and Water Images
Mirror and Water Images.content: Identify the mirror or water image of a given figure or word.

Paper Folding/Cutting.title: Paper Folding/Cutting
Paper Folding/Cutting.content: Visualize the result of folding/cutting paper and predict the outcome.

Series completion using figures.title: Series completion using figures
Series completion using figures.content: Find the next figure in a sequence based on the pattern.

Clocks and Calendars.title: Clocks and Calendars
Clocks and Calendars.content: Problems based on calculation of time, angles, and dates.

Ranking and Order.title: Ranking and Order
Ranking and Order.content: Questions involving ranking of people or objects based on given information.

Data Sufficiency.title: Data Sufficiency
Data Sufficiency.content: Determine if the given data is sufficient to answer the question.

English Language & Comprehension.title: English Language & Comprehension
English Language & Comprehension.overview: Verbal ability tests language proficiency and comprehension.

Paragraphs.title: Comprehension Skills
Paragraphs.content: Practice reading passages to understand the main idea, draw logical inferences, and recognize the author's tone (e.g., critical, humorous, neutral).

Synonyms.title: Synonyms
Synonyms.content: Words with similar meanings. Example: Happy - Joyful.

Antonyms.title: Antonyms
Antonyms.content: Words with opposite meanings. Example: Hot - Cold.

Cloze Test (fillers in a paragraph).title: Cloze Test
Cloze Test (fillers in a paragraph).content: Fill in the blanks in a passage with appropriate words based on context.

Idioms/Phrases.title: Idioms/Phrases
Idioms/Phrases.content: Common expressions with non-literal meanings. Example: 'Break the ice'.

Error Spotting.title: Error Spotting
Error Spotting.content: Identify grammatical or usage errors in sentences.

Sentence Improvement.title: Sentence Improvement
Sentence Improvement.content: Choose the best version of a sentence from the given options.

Fill in the Blanks.title: Fill in the Blanks
Fill in the Blanks.content: Complete sentences with the most suitable words.

Data Interpretation (DI).title: Data Interpretation (DI)
Data Interpretation (DI).overview: Data Interpretation involves analyzing and drawing conclusions from data presented in various forms such as tables, graphs, and charts.

Tabular Data.title: Tabular Data Analysis
Tabular Data.content:
Analysis of Tables

Tabular data is the most common form of DI. The key is to identify intersections of rows and columns quickly to perform calculations.

Percentage Share = (Component Value / Total Sum) × 100

Visual Graphs.title: Line and Bar Charts
Visual Graphs.content:
Graphical Representation

Visual graphs allow for easy comparison. Bar graphs are ideal for discrete quantities, while line graphs are best for identifying trends over time.

Focus on peaks and valleys in line graphs to identify growth cycles.

Caselets.title: Paragraph-Based DI
Caselets.content:
Descriptive Information

In Caselets, data is hidden within text. The most efficient strategy is to convert the paragraph into a table as you read.
